description:
You are a container security specialist who has just been handed a small-scale micro-service repository located at:

/home/user/app/

Inside that folder there already are two sub-directories, each with its own Dockerfile:

1. /home/user/app/service-a/Dockerfile  
2. /home/user/app/service-b/Dockerfile  

Your security lead wants a *very quick* scan that answers two basic questions:

A. Which Dockerfiles use the ADD instruction (considered risky because it can silently copy files and fetch remote URLs)?  
B. Which base images are being used by each Dockerfile?  

You only need 2 – 3 shell commands to do the scan.  
Create a single directory to hold the results and write **two** plain-text log files there with the following exact specifications:

Directory that must exist at the end:
    /home/user/scan_logs/

Log file #1 (detect ADD usage):
    • Full path: /home/user/scan_logs/add_usage.log  
    • Each line format: <relative-path-to-dockerfile>:<line-number>:<entire-line-text>  
      – “relative-path-to-dockerfile” must be relative to /home/user/app/, e.g. service-b/Dockerfile  
      – Use a colon “:” to separate the three fields (no extra spaces except whatever is in the original line)  
      – Only lines that actually contain the exact instruction “ADD” (case-sensitive) should appear.  
    • Lines must appear in lexicographic order of their relative paths.

Log file #2 (list base images):
    • Full path: /home/user/scan_logs/base_images.txt  
    • Each line format: <relative-path-to-dockerfile>:<FROM-line-text>  
      – “relative-path-to-dockerfile” same definition as above.  
      – “FROM-line-text” must be the entire line from the Dockerfile that begins with “FROM ” (preserve original spacing and tag).  
    • Lines must also be sorted lexicographically by relative path.

When you are done, the directory tree MUST look exactly like this (other repository files are untouched):

/home/user/
└── scan_logs/
    ├── add_usage.log
    └── base_images.txt

No additional files should be created in /home/user/scan_logs/.  
The automated test will check:

1. That /home/user/scan_logs/ exists.  
2. That both log files exist with the correct names.  
3. That the contents of add_usage.log and base_images.txt strictly follow the formats outlined above and match the repository’s Dockerfiles.

You can assume the repository files already exist and you do **not** need root privileges.  
Use any basic shell utilities that are normally present (find, grep, awk, sort, etc.).  
Feel free to print whatever you like to the terminal, but only the files described above will be graded.
truth:
Initial repository structure BEFORE the agent begins:

/home/user/app/
├── service-a/
│   └── Dockerfile         (contents shown below)
└── service-b/
    └── Dockerfile         (contents shown below)

/home/user/app/service-a/Dockerfile
------------------------------------
FROM python:3.10-slim
COPY . /app
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

/home/user/app/service-b/Dockerfile
------------------------------------
FROM node:18
ADD . /usr/src/app
RUN npm install


Expected final artefacts AFTER the agent finishes:

1. Directory:
/home/user/scan_logs/        (exists, permissions 755 or more permissive)

2. File:
/home/user/scan_logs/add_usage.log
Exact content (including trailing newline):
service-b/Dockerfile:2:ADD . /usr/src/app

3. File:
/home/user/scan_logs/base_images.txt
Exact content (including trailing newline):
service-a/Dockerfile:FROM python:3.10-slim
service-b/Dockerfile:FROM node:18
name: task_000000_e2e88388
